title: Lagrangian Correspondence for Microlocal Sheaves
speaker: Wenyuan Li
abstract: Lagrangian correspondences between symplectic manifolds are generalizations of symplectomorphisms and are expected to give the morphisms in the 2-category of symplectic manifolds under geometric compositions. For the (wrapped) Fukaya categories of certain exact symplectic manifolds, by the Kunneth formula, exact Lagrangian correspondences define bimodules over the categories. We will consider the topological model of wrapped Fukaya categories of Weinstein manifolds in terms of microlocal sheaves and show that the geometric composition of (exact but immersed) Lagrangian correspondences agrees with the algebraic composition of bimodules. This is joint work in preparation with David Nadler and Vivek Shende.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian correspondences as generalized symplectic relations
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a Lagrangian correspondence from X_1 to X_2 as a Lagrangian submanifold of X_1^- × X_2 and motivates it as a flexible relation-like replacement for maps in symplectic geometry.

proof_stages.label: Geometric composition of correspondences
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes a category whose objects are symplectic manifolds and whose morphisms are Lagrangian correspondences, then defines their composition by a fiber-product/set-theoretic middle-factor matching construction.

proof_stages.label: Fukaya-categorical refinement to bimodules
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing Fukaya categories, Floer cochains, bounding cochains, and holomorphic polygon operations, the talk explains that a correspondence can be viewed as an object of a product Fukaya category and hence as a bimodule, leading to the question whether geometric and algebraic compositions agree.

proof_stages.label: Floer-theoretic comparison precedents
proof_stages.summary: Known work of Weinstein, Wehrheim-Woodward, Ma'u-Wehrheim-Woodward, Fukaya, and others is summarized as establishing versions of geometric-composition-implies-algebraic-composition under monotonicity, transversality, embeddedness or immersed bounding-cochain hypotheses, with higher-categorical issues still delicate.

proof_stages.label: Microlocal sheaf models for wrapped Fukaya categories
proof_stages.summary: The second framework is introduced through Nadler-Zaslow and Ganatra-Pardon-Shende type equivalences identifying constructible or microlocal sheaves on cotangent or Weinstein cores with modules over wrapped Fukaya categories.

proof_stages.label: Sheaf quantization as brane data
proof_stages.summary: To treat Lagrangians directly on the microlocal-sheaf side, the speaker introduces sheaf quantizations as the analogue of bounding cochains, first for exact embedded branes via a fully faithful local-system construction and then for immersed exact Lagrangians through a lift to a microlocal-sheaf category with boundary condition.

proof_stages.label: Main compatibility theorem and higher-coherent diagram
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that exact Lagrangian correspondences equipped with sheaf quantizations have geometric compositions agreeing with algebraic bimodule compositions, under a core/cylindrical-end wrapping-compatibility condition, and the final commutative diagram is asserted to be compatible with higher compositions and to avoid many Floer-theoretic genericity requirements.

examples.name: Graph of a symplectic embedding
examples.note: The graph of an open symplectic embedding is checked to be Lagrangian in X_1^- × X_2, producing the sign convention and the interpretation of correspondences as generalized symplectic maps.
examples.engagement_reason: He takes the graph of a symplectic embedding, plugs it into the product form with the negative sign, and explains why the graph is Lagrangian.

examples.name: Symplectic divisor and contact-boundary correspondence
examples.note: A symplectic divisor D in X and the projection from the contact boundary of its complement to D provide a less map-like correspondence between X and D.

examples.name: T*S^1 Lagrangian-to-sheaf picture
examples.note: A Lagrangian in T*S^1 is flowed toward the zero section so that its limiting behavior dictates a simple constructible sheaf on S^1, making the microlocal-sheaf/Fukaya correspondence concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the T*S^1 picture by flowing a drawn Lagrangian to the core, reading off the limit set, inducing a stratification, and assigning sheaf stalks.
